summary: Joel observes that Microsoft appears to maintain a dedicated marketing team whose sole purpose is to invent and promote meaningless technology buzzwords each year. He traces a pattern from WOSA to Intellisense to ActiveX to DNA to NGWS, noting that none of these terms represent real, concrete products. The post also includes housekeeping notes about adding a site index and progress on his 'UI for Programmers' book.
key_points: Microsoft cyclically invents meaningless technology brand names to market aggressively, creating buzz around non-products
The pattern of vaporware branding includes WOSA, Intellisense, ActiveX, DNA, and NGWS across successive years
These branded initiatives share a common trait: they don't represent tangible products or meaningful technical concepts
money_quotes: I'm coming to suspect that Microsoft has this one extra marketing team that is not assigned to a product. Their job is to make up a non-thing and market the heck out of it.
What all these have in common is that they don't exist and don't mean anything, but they have a marketing team from Microsoft blathering about them.
key_insight: Microsoft's recurring strategy of inventing and heavily marketing abstract technology buzzwords that don't correspond to real products reveals how tech industry marketing often prioritizes narrative over substance.
